limitations: This analysis is based on summarized operational evidence and retained calibration snapshots.
Intermediate intraday behavior is reconstructed from snapshots, so unobserved trigger timing should be treated conservatively.

executiveSummary.headline: TEPCO outperformed the model on both level and shape
executiveSummary.summary: The model underperformed on 16 of 24 comparable hours and had higher error on all headline metrics: MAE 380.2 MW vs 272.1 MW, WAPE 1.30% vs 0.93%, and RMSE 484.3 MW vs 318.8 MW. The main operational weakness was late-afternoon/evening shape error, especially the 17-18 break and the 18:00 miss.

rootCauseHypotheses.title: Late-afternoon ramp reversal was not tracked
rootCauseHypotheses.explanation: The largest shape break was 17-18, where actual demand fell 230 MW while the model rose 1,352.3 MW. This aligned with the worst hour at 18:00, where model error reached 1,134.1 MW.
counterEvidence: TEPCO also missed the same transition, but by a much smaller amount.
No calibration flag shows a transition-specific correction was applied.

rootCauseHypotheses.title: Morning ramp may be contaminated by prior-day inertia
rootCauseHypotheses.explanation: Hours 06-10 showed positive lag-24 same-business-type gaps and the diagnostic mean was 338.0 MW, while model bias stayed negative at -148.0 MW. Because the period includes a business-day to non-business-day transition signal in the diagnostics, lag-24 inertia is plausible.
counterEvidence: The time-band verdict for 06-10 was only close, not a clear failure.
Direct business-type transition application was not observed in calibration facts.

rootCauseHypotheses.title: Positive residual damping was absent despite a strong rising bias
rootCauseHypotheses.explanation: The model showed positive bias in daytime and late afternoon, including 279.0 MW in 11-15 and 317.4 MW in 16-18. Calibration facts show positiveResidualMitigationApplied=false and positiveResidualSlopeDampingApplied=false, so the forecast did not dampen the rising residual shape.
counterEvidence: The calibration layer did not record a positive-residual damping application, but absence alone is not proof the trigger should have fired.
The strongest miss also involves a shape reversal, not only level bias.

featureRecommendations.suggestion: Activate damping when 16-18 residual slope turns more positive than +200 MW/h for two consecutive hours; cap the adjustment at 300 MW.
featureRecommendations.expectedEffect: Lower late-afternoon overshoot and reduce 17-18 shape breaks.
featureRecommendations.risk: Could underreact to genuine load inflections.
featureRecommendations.validationPlan: Replay the retained calibration snapshots and require lower 16-18 MAE without worsening 06-10 by more than 25 MW.

featureRecommendations.suggestion: Apply a transition prior only for 06-10 when lag24 same-business-type gap exceeds +250 MW and the day is a business-day to non-business-day switch.
featureRecommendations.expectedEffect: Reduce morning ramp contamination from prior-day inertia.
featureRecommendations.risk: May overcorrect on stable ramp days.
featureRecommendations.validationPlan: Replay days with transition labels and compare 06-10 bias and MAE against the current baseline.

operatorNotes: TEPCO held the edge in overnight, late afternoon, and evening, while the model was only slightly better in 11-15 and tied nowhere.
The calibration snapshot was applied with observed hours 22 and baseAdjustmentMw -357.6, but no business-type transition or residual damping flag was applied.
The published-versus-recalculated gap at 21:00 was -878.0 MW, which is within a freeze-gap context and indicates a materially different latest recalculated line than the published forecast.
limitations: This analysis is based on summarized operational evidence and retained calibration snapshots.
Intermediate intraday behavior is reconstructed from snapshots, so unobserved trigger timing should be treated conservatively.
